story:
Every night, Mrs. Briton would slip out of the house at exactly 1:00 a.m., moving so softly that even the floorboards did not dare complain. She kept her secret like a locked drawer: most of her family believed the woman they leaned on was held together by tiny vials and a hypodermic needle. In the woods, under the trees that kept her confidence, she would push back her sleeve and inject herself—sometimes colorless, sometimes tinged blue, sometimes red. Then she would return and go on being who she was. Or who she needed to be.

It worked until it didn’t. One night the vials ran empty. Panic beat at her ribs. She rushed to the all-night pharmacy, the one that had never failed her, only to find a yellow X of police tape across the glass door. The place was sealed and silent. A sour, heavy smell drifted out through a crack in the frame. She edged closer, and her eyes caught a pale, sagging face inside—familiar features slackening in decay. The pharmacist. The recognition hit her like cold water. She clapped a hand over her mouth and ran.

Branches clawed at her sleeves as she tore back into the dark. Owls called. Insects sang. The path tilted. A tree loomed up out of nowhere. She swerved and lost her footing. The hill gave way beneath her, and she tumbled through brush and rock, feeling skin tear as the world went sharp and bright. At the bottom she came to rest in a slick of her own blood, breath ragged, vision stuttering.

Then the night changed. The red smears she had left on bark and stone began to move. Thick streaks of blood lifted and slithered, pulling themselves together into serpents that hissed and tasted the air with red tongues. They slid toward her, over her, leaving burning trails where they touched. She tried to kick them away, but her legs felt slow, heavy, useless. Something cracked—branch or bone, she couldn’t tell—and the sound rang in her head as the world went black.

She woke in her own bed, light pressing at the edges of the curtains. Her son’s arms were around her, steadying, warm. He looked at her the way you look at someone you love who won’t stop standing on the edge. “I told you, Mom,” he said quietly. “Keep extra medicine. Please. Before you get hurt trying to run from your own hallucinations.”

Only then did the pieces settle into place. The secret trips. The injections that kept her mind orderly. The terror in the woods when she had none left. The dead pharmacist—real enough to explain the police tape and the smell—and the snakes that were not real at all. She had been fighting a condition that, untreated, could twist the world into threats. Hiding it had seemed safer. It wasn’t. The medicine was not weakness; it was the thin bridge between her and the dark. She lay still, listening to her son breathe, and understood what she would have to do to keep going: prepare, tell the truth, and stop running from what lived inside her.
